title: Polls Open in Razor-Thin Denmark Election Under Shadow of Trump's Greenland Threats
description: Danish voters head to the polls in a tightly contested election, with incumbent PM Mette Frederiksen vying to keep power amidst domestic issues and a recent geopolitical crisis over Greenland.
lede: Danish voters are casting their ballots today in what is expected to be a razor-thin general election. Incumbent Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen is fighting to maintain power, but polls suggest neither major political bloc will secure an outright majority.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Polls opened across Denmark and its semiautonomous territory, Greenland, early Tuesday morning. Voters are participating in a highly anticipated general election that could reshape the country's political landscape.

Incumbent Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen, representing the Social Democrats, is widely predicted to retain her position. However, final polling indicates an extremely tight race, with neither the left-wing 'red' bloc nor the right-wing 'blue' bloc expected to win a clear majority.
The newly formed center-right Moderates party, led by former two-time Prime Minister Lars Løkke Rasmussen, is poised to become the pivotal kingmaker in any upcoming coalition negotiations.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The election results will dictate Denmark's trajectory on critical domestic issues, including the cost of living, animal welfare, and drinking water quality. Furthermore, the newly elected government will be responsible for steering the nation's ongoing, hardline immigration policies.

Internationally, the outcome carries weight as Europe navigates shifting geopolitical landscapes. Pelle Dragsted, leader of the Red-Green Alliance, emphasized the election's broader significance, framing it as part of a continent-wide clash against authoritarian forces.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The election takes place against a backdrop of recent geopolitical tension, notably a crisis in January sparked by US President Donald Trump's repeated threats to take control of Greenland. This incident led to a temporary surge in support for Frederiksen, dubbed the 'Greenland bump'.

Domestically, Frederiksen's tenure has been marked by a rightward shift on immigration within the center-left. Analysts note that her government's stringent policies have effectively stalled the growth of far-right parties like the Danish People's Party, but at the cost of normalizing anti-immigrant rhetoric in mainstream political discourse.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Polls are scheduled to close at 8:00 PM local time. Exit polls and initial results are expected shortly after, which will provide the first concrete indications of the new parliamentary makeup.

If predictions hold true, the immediate aftermath of the election will likely see intense, behind-the-scenes negotiations. Lars Løkke Rasmussen has already positioned himself to act as a 'royal investigator' to explore potential centrist coalition agreements, signaling a potentially protracted government formation process.
